data.content:
### Good Context Examples for Agent Triggering

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/agent-development/references/triggering-examples.md

Examples of well-written context statements that set the scene for agent triggering. Good contexts are specific and describe the exact situation that led to the interaction.

```markdown
Context: User just implemented a new authentication feature
Context: User has created a PR and wants it reviewed
Context: User is debugging a test failure
Context: After writing several functions without documentation
```

--------------------------------

### Load Project-Specific Context using Claude Code Hooks

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/hook-development/references/patterns.md

This pattern uses a `SessionStart` hook to automatically load project-specific context at the beginning of a session. It executes a bash script to detect the project type (e.g., Node.js, Rust) and sets environment variables accordingly, streamlining setup.

```json
{
  "SessionStart": [
    {
      "matcher": "*",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "command",
          "command": "bash ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/load-context.sh"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
cd "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" || exit 1

# Detect project type
if [ -f "package.json" ]; then
  echo "📦 Node.js project detected"
  echo "export PROJECT_TYPE=nodejs" >> "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"
elif [ -f "Cargo.toml" ]; then
  echo "🦀 Rust project detected"
  echo "export PROJECT_TYPE=rust" >> "$CLAUDE_ENV_FILE"
fi
```

--------------------------------

### Configure SessionStart Hook with Bash Command

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/hook-development/SKILL.md

Executes when Claude Code session begins to load context and set environment using a bash script. Demonstrates command hook type with plugin root path variable for portability.

```json
{
  "SessionStart": [
    {
      "matcher": "*",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "command",
          "command": "bash ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/load-context.sh"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

--------------------------------

### Implement Context-Aware Prompt Hooks for Full Transcript Review (JSON)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/hook-development/references/advanced.md

A JSON configuration for a 'Stop' event prompt hook that leverages comprehensive session context. The prompt instructs the LLM to review the full transcript (`$TRANSCRIPT_PATH`) and evaluate multiple criteria—like test execution, build success, question answering, and unfinished work—before approving the task. This enables highly intelligent, context-driven decisions.

```json
{
  "Stop": [
    {
      "matcher": "*",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "prompt",
          "prompt": "Review the full transcript at $TRANSCRIPT_PATH. Check: 1) Were tests run after code changes? 2) Did the build succeed? 3) Were all user questions answered? 4) Is there any unfinished work? Return 'approve' only if everything is complete."
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Advanced Prompt Hook for LLM-based Command Validation (JSON)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/hook-development/references/migration.md

This JSON configuration sets up an advanced prompt hook, leveraging an LLM for dynamic command validation before tool use. It provides a natural language prompt to the LLM to analyze commands for destructive operations, privilege escalation, and network activity, expecting an 'approve' or 'deny' decision with an explanation, offering greater flexibility and context awareness.

```json
{
  "PreToolUse": [
    {
      "matcher": "Bash",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "prompt",
          "prompt": "Command: $TOOL_INPUT.command. Analyze for: 1) Destructive operations (rm -rf, dd, mkfs, etc) 2) Privilege escalation (sudo) 3) Network operations without user consent. Return 'approve' or 'deny' with explanation.",
          "timeout": 15
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

--------------------------------

### Error Handling Pattern with Logging in JavaScript

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/plugin-structure/examples/standard-plugin.md

Demonstrates proper error handling with context logging, specific error types, and resource cleanup. This pattern ensures errors are never silently swallowed and provides actionable error messages for debugging and monitoring purposes.

```javascript
async function processData(data) {
  try {
    const result = await transform(data)
    return result
  } catch (error) {
    logger.error('Data processing failed', {
      data: sanitize(data),
      error: error.message,
      stack: error.stack
    })
    throw new DataProcessingError('Failed to process data', { cause: error })
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for Relative Paths

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/command-development/examples/plugin-commands.md

Demonstrates the correct way to reference plugin scripts using environment variables instead of relative paths. Relative paths can break depending on execution context, while ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} provides a reliable absolute reference across different installations.

```markdown
# Wrong
!`node ./scripts/analyze.js`

# Correct
!`node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/analyze.js`
```

--------------------------------

### Standard Example Block Format for Agent Triggering

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/agent-development/references/triggering-examples.md

The standard markdown format for writing example blocks that trigger agents. This template shows the complete structure with context, user message, assistant response, commentary, and agent triggering line.

```markdown
<example>
Context: [Describe the situation - what led to this interaction]
user: "[Exact user message or request]"
assistant: "[How Claude should respond before triggering]"
<commentary>
[Explanation of why this agent should be triggered in this scenario]
</commentary>
assistant: "[How Claude triggers the agent - usually 'I'll use the [agent-name] agent...']"
</example>
```

--------------------------------

### Conceptual SessionStart Hook for Explanatory Output Style

Source: https://context7.com/anthropics/claude-code/llms.txt

This conceptual hook, automatically activated at the start of a session, aims to enhance Claude's output by providing educational insights. It helps explain the 'why' behind certain implementation choices, discusses trade-offs between different approaches, highlights project-specific best practices, and offers historical context for architectural decisions, mimicking a deprecated explanatory output style.

```bash
# Automatically activated at session start
# Adds educational insights about implementation choices
# Mimics the deprecated Explanatory output style

# Provides context on:
# - Why certain patterns are used in the codebase
# - Trade-offs between different approaches
# - Best practices for the project's tech stack
# - Historical context for architectural decisions
```

--------------------------------

### Define Claude Code Agent Generation System Prompt

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/agent-development/references/agent-creation-system-prompt.md

This system prompt guides an elite AI agent architect in translating user requirements into precisely-tuned agent specifications. It outlines a detailed six-step process including extracting core intent, designing an expert persona, architecting comprehensive instructions, optimizing for performance, creating a unique identifier, and providing example usage scenarios. The prompt emphasizes alignment with project-specific context (e.g., CLAUDE.md files) and requires the output to be a valid JSON object defining the agent's identifier, 'whenToUse' conditions with examples, and its 'systemPrompt'.

```plaintext
You are an elite AI agent architect specializing in crafting high-performance agent configurations. Your expertise lies in translating user requirements into precisely-tuned agent specifications that maximize effectiveness and reliability.

**Important Context**: You may have access to project-specific instructions from CLAUDE.md files and other context that may include coding standards, project structure, and custom requirements. Consider this context when creating agents to ensure they align with the project's established patterns and practices.

When a user describes what they want an agent to do, you will:

1. **Extract Core Intent**: Identify the fundamental purpose, key responsibilities, and success criteria for the agent. Look for both explicit requirements and implicit needs. Consider any project-specific context from CLAUDE.md files. For agents that are meant to review code, you should assume that the user is asking to review recently written code and not the whole codebase, unless the user has explicitly instructed you otherwise.

2. **Design Expert Persona**: Create a compelling expert identity that embodies deep domain knowledge relevant to the task. The persona should inspire confidence and guide the agent's decision-making approach.

3. **Architect Comprehensive Instructions**: Develop a system prompt that:
   - Establishes clear behavioral boundaries and operational parameters
   - Provides specific methodologies and best practices for task execution
   - Anticipates edge cases and provides guidance for handling them
   - Incorporates any specific requirements or preferences mentioned by the user
   - Defines output format expectations when relevant
   - Aligns with project-specific coding standards and patterns from CLAUDE.md

4. **Optimize for Performance**: Include:
   - Decision-making frameworks appropriate to the domain
   - Quality control mechanisms and self-verification steps
   - Efficient workflow patterns
   - Clear escalation or fallback strategies

5. **Create Identifier**: Design a concise, descriptive identifier that:
   - Uses lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only
   - Is typically 2-4 words joined by hyphens
   - Clearly indicates the agent's primary function
   - Is memorable and easy to type
   - Avoids generic terms like "helper" or "assistant"

6. **Example agent descriptions**:
   - In the 'whenToUse' field of the JSON object, you should include examples of when this agent should be used.
   - Examples should be of the form:
     <example>
     Context: The user is creating a code-review agent that should be called after a logical chunk of code is written.
     user: "Please write a function that checks if a number is prime"
     assistant: "Here is the relevant function: "
     <function call omitted for brevity only for this example>
     <commentary>
     Since a logical chunk of code was written and the task was completed, now use the code-review agent to review the code.
     </commentary>
     assistant: "Now let me use the code-reviewer agent to review the code"
     </example>
   - If the user mentioned or implied that the agent should be used proactively, you should include examples of this.
   - NOTE: Ensure that in the examples, you are making the assistant use the Agent tool and not simply respond directly to the task.

Your output must be a valid JSON object with exactly these fields:
{
  "identifier": "A unique, descriptive identifier using lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens (e.g., 'code-reviewer', 'api-docs-writer', 'test-generator')",
  "whenToUse": "A precise, actionable description starting with 'Use this agent when...' that clearly defines the triggering conditions and use cases. Ensure you include examples as described above.",
  "systemPrompt": "The complete system prompt that will govern the agent's behavior, written in second person ('You are...', 'You will...') and structured for maximum clarity and effectiveness"
}

Key principles for your system prompts:
- Be specific rather than generic - avoid vague instructions
- Include concrete examples when they would clarify behavior
- Balance comprehensiveness with clarity - every instruction should add value
- Ensure the agent has enough context to handle variations of the core task
- Make the agent proactive in seeking clarification when needed
- Build in quality assurance and self-correction mechanisms

Remember: The agents you create should be autonomous experts capable of handling their designated tasks with minimal additional guidance. Your system prompts are their complete operational manual.
```

--------------------------------

### Example Prompt-Based PreToolUse Hook Configuration (JSON)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/hook-development/SKILL.md

This example configures a `PreToolUse` hook using a prompt-based approach to validate file write operations. It specifies a matcher for 'Write' or 'Edit' actions and provides a detailed prompt instructing the LLM to check for security vulnerabilities. This hook runs before any tool executes, allowing for dynamic approval or denial of operations based on context.

```json
{
  "PreToolUse": [
    {
      "matcher": "Write|Edit",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "prompt",
          "prompt": "Validate file write safety. Check: system paths, credentials, path traversal, sensitive content. Return 'approve' or 'deny'."
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

--------------------------------

### Install Claude Code on Windows using PowerShell

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/README.md

Instructions for installing Claude Code on Windows operating systems using PowerShell. The command uses `Invoke-RestMethod` (irm) to download and execute an installation script directly from the web.

```powershell
irm https://claude.ai/install.ps1 | iex
```

--------------------------------

### Context-Aware Operation Handler - Bash

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/command-development/references/documentation-patterns.md

Validates that required operation parameters are provided and displays contextual help with available operations, usage patterns, and examples. This approach guides users by showing relevant options only when needed, improving command discoverability.

```bash
if [ -z "$1" ]; then
  echo "No operation specified"
  echo ""
  echo "Available operations:"
  echo "- analyze: Analyze target for issues"
  echo "- fix: Apply automatic fixes"
  echo "- report: Generate detailed report"
  echo ""
  echo "Usage: /command [operation] [target]"
  echo ""
  echo "Examples:"
  echo "  /command analyze src/"
  echo "  /command fix src/app.js"
  echo "  /command report"
  echo ""
  echo "Run /command help for more details."
  exit 1
fi
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Kubernetes Pod Security Context

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/plugin-structure/examples/advanced-plugin.md

Apply security contexts to pods to enforce best practices and enhance security by reducing attack surface. This example demonstrates how to run a container as a non-root user, set a specific user ID, make the root filesystem read-only, and drop all unnecessary Linux capabilities.

```yaml
securityContext:
  runAsNonRoot: true
  runAsUser: 1000
  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
  capabilities:
    drop:
      - ALL
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Prompt-Based Hook for LLM Decision Making (JSON)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/hook-development/SKILL.md

This configuration defines a prompt-based hook that leverages an LLM for context-aware decision making. It specifies the hook type as 'prompt', includes the natural language prompt for evaluation, and sets a timeout. These hooks are ideal for flexible logic and better handling of edge cases without complex scripting.

```json
{
  "type": "prompt",
  "prompt": "Evaluate if this tool use is appropriate: $TOOL_INPUT",
  "timeout": 30
}
```

--------------------------------

### Avoid Monolithic SKILL.md Files

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/skill-development/SKILL.md

Illustrates an undesirable skill file structure where all content is crammed into a single, large SKILL.md file. This approach bloats the context loaded when the skill is triggered, violating progressive disclosure principles and potentially impacting performance.

```File Structure
skill-name/
└── SKILL.md  (8,000 words - everything in one file)
```

--------------------------------

### Initialize Ralph Loop State File with Markdown Frontmatter

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/plugin-settings/references/real-world-examples.md

Creates the initial state file with YAML frontmatter containing loop configuration (iteration count, max iterations, completion promise, timestamp) and the user prompt in the markdown body. Establishes the foundation for loop state persistence and continuation.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
PROMPT="$1"
MAX_ITERATIONS="${2:-0}"
COMPLETION_PROMISE="${3:-}"

cat > ".claude/ralph-loop.local.md" <<EOF
---
iteration: 1
max_iterations: $MAX_ITERATIONS
completion_promise: "$COMPLETION_PROMISE"
started_at: "$(date -Iseconds)"
---

$PROMPT
EOF

echo "Ralph loop initialized: .claude/ralph-loop.local.md"
```

--------------------------------

### Individual Agent Trigger Examples

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/pr-review-toolkit/README.md

Example queries that automatically trigger the appropriate review agents based on the question context. Each query demonstrates how to invoke specific agents for targeted code review.

```text
"Can you check if the tests cover all edge cases?"
→ Triggers pr-test-analyzer

"Review the error handling in the API client"
→ Triggers silent-failure-hunter

"I've added documentation - is it accurate?"
→ Triggers comment-analyzer
```

--------------------------------

### Create Agent Component Structure

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/plugin-structure/SKILL.md

Directory structure for agent components located in agents/ directory. Agents are Markdown files with YAML frontmatter that auto-discover and can be invoked manually or automatically by Claude Code based on task context.

```text
agents/
├── code-reviewer.md
├── test-generator.md
└── refactorer.md
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Advanced Prompt Hook for File Write and Edit Validation (JSON)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/hook-development/references/migration.md

This JSON configuration establishes an advanced prompt hook for validating file write or edit operations using an LLM. It provides the file path and content preview to the LLM, instructing it to verify against criteria like system directories, credential exposure, and path traversal, offering a flexible and context-aware validation mechanism.

```json
{
  "PreToolUse": [
    {
      "matcher": "Write|Edit",
      "hooks": [
        {
          "type": "prompt",
          "prompt": "File path: $TOOL_INPUT.file_path. Content preview: $TOOL_INPUT.content (first 200 chars). Verify: 1) Not system directories (/etc, /sys, /usr) 2) Not credentials (.env, tokens, secrets) 3) No path traversal 4) Content doesn't expose secrets. Return 'approve' or 'deny'."
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

--------------------------------

### Cancel Ralph Loop Command Usage (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/ralph-wiggum/commands/help.md

The `/cancel-ralph` command is used to terminate an active Ralph loop. It removes the state file that maintains the loop's context, providing a clean way to stop the iterative process.

```bash
/cancel-ralph
```

--------------------------------

### Handle Empty and Null Field Values in Bash

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/plugin-settings/references/parsing-techniques.md

Safely extracts field values and provides default fallback for empty or null entries. Checks for both empty strings and literal 'null' values. Useful for optional configuration fields that may be missing or explicitly set to null.

```bash
VALUE=$(echo "$FRONTMATTER" | grep '^field1:' | sed 's/field1: */''/)
# VALUE will be empty string

# Check for empty/null
if [[ -z "$VALUE" ]] || [[ "$VALUE" == "null" ]]; then
  VALUE="default"
fi
```

--------------------------------

### Prompt Best Practice: Defining Clear Completion Criteria (Markdown)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/ralph-wiggum/README.md

This example demonstrates how to write an effective prompt for the Ralph loop by including explicit and measurable completion criteria. A well-defined prompt ensures the AI understands precisely when a task is considered finished, including specific requirements like CRUD operations, validation, test coverage, and documentation.

```markdown
Build a REST API for todos.

When complete:
- All CRUD endpoints working
- Input validation in place
- Tests passing (coverage > 80%)
- README with API docs
- Output: <promise>COMPLETE</promise>
```

--------------------------------

### Complex Choices Pattern Using Arguments and Questions

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/command-development/references/interactive-commands.md

Shows a workflow that accepts project name as an argument, then uses AskUserQuestion for complex architectural and deployment decisions. This pattern separates simple inputs from choices requiring detailed explanations and context.

```markdown
Project name from argument: $1

Now use AskUserQuestion to choose:
- Architecture pattern
- Technology stack
- Deployment strategy

These require explanation, so questions work better than arguments.
```

--------------------------------

### Prompt Best Practice: Structuring Incremental Goals (Markdown)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/ralph-wiggum/README.md

This snippet illustrates the best practice of breaking down large tasks into smaller, manageable phases within a Ralph loop prompt. By defining incremental goals, the AI can focus on completing one part at a time, making complex projects more approachable and trackable.

```markdown
Phase 1: User authentication (JWT, tests)
Phase 2: Product catalog (list/search, tests)
Phase 3: Shopping cart (add/remove, tests)

Output <promise>COMPLETE</promise> when all phases done.
```

--------------------------------

### Configure MCP Integration with .mcp.json

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/commands/create-plugin.md

JSON configuration file for Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration that specifies server type (stdio for local, SSE for hosted), command execution, arguments with environment variable substitution, and optional language mappings for LSP servers.

```json
{
  "serverType": "stdio",
  "command": "node",
  "args": ["${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/server.js"],
  "extensionToLanguage": {},
  "env": {}
}
```

--------------------------------

### MCP Servers Configuration - JSON

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/plugin-structure/examples/advanced-plugin.md

Configures three MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers for Kubernetes, Terraform, and GitHub Actions integration. Each server defines its command, arguments, and environment variables required for execution and authentication.

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "kubernetes": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/servers/kubernetes-mcp/index.js"],
      "env": {
        "KUBECONFIG": "${KUBECONFIG}",
        "K8S_NAMESPACE": "${K8S_NAMESPACE:-default}"
      }
    },
    "terraform": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/servers/terraform-mcp/main.py"],
      "env": {
        "TF_STATE_BUCKET": "${TF_STATE_BUCKET}",
        "AWS_REGION": "${AWS_REGION}"
      }
    },
    "github-actions": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/servers/github-actions-mcp/server.js"],
      "env": {
        "GITHUB_TOKEN": "${GITHUB_TOKEN}",
        "GITHUB_ORG": "${GITHUB_ORG}"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Prompt Best Practice: Implementing Iteration Limits and Escape Hatches (Bash/Markdown)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/ralph-wiggum/README.md

This snippet combines a `bash` command with a `markdown` prompt example to illustrate the crucial use of `--max-iterations` as a safety mechanism. It demonstrates how to prevent infinite loops by setting an iteration limit and how to instruct the AI on documenting blockers or suggesting alternatives if the task remains incomplete.

```bash
# Recommended: Always set a reasonable iteration limit
/ralph-loop "Try to implement feature X" --max-iterations 20
```

```markdown
# In your prompt, include what to do if stuck:
# "After 15 iterations, if not complete:
#  - Document what's blocking progress
#  - List what was attempted
#  - Suggest alternative approaches"
```

--------------------------------

### Set Environment Variables for User Configuration (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/mcp-integration/references/authentication.md

Provides bash commands to set `WORKSPACE_ID` and `TENANT_ID` environment variables. These variables can then be used by applications or plugins to configure per-user or per-workspace settings, enabling dynamic behavior based on the user's context.

```bash
export WORKSPACE_ID="my-workspace-123"
export TENANT_ID="my-company"
```

--------------------------------

### Interactive Workflow Command with Invocation Disabled

Source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/main/plugins/plugin-dev/skills/command-development/references/frontmatter-reference.md

SlashCommand configured with disable-model-invocation for interactive workflows requiring user input. Ensures setup wizards and guided processes cannot be automated by the model.

```markdown
---
description: Walk through setup wizard
disable-model-invocation: true
---
```
